meta.author: Leana Le
meta.purpose: Portfolio case studies with prototype-aware content
meta.notes: All projects are student projects. No fabricated metrics. Prototypes appear after their referenced sections.

case_studies.title: ProLog
case_studies.subtitle: A journey companion for neurodivergent apprentices
case_studies.summary: A centralized apprenticeship companion helping neurodivergent apprentices track progress, reduce cognitive overload, and build confidence through clear visualization and structured tools.
case_studies.context: School project in collaboration with ConnectHER at BCIT

hook.content:
Apprenticeship can take over 6,000 hours. That's years of work, spread across job sites, classrooms, and bureaucratic systems—often without a clear sense of whether you're on track.

Early in our research, we kept hearing the same thing: confusion was pushing people out. Not inability. Not lack of motivation. Just confusion about where they stood and what came next.

We wanted to build something that answered one question clearly: Where am I right now?

problem.content:
The apprenticeship system fragments critical information across three platforms. Hours, competencies, certifications—all siloed.

For neurodivergent apprentices navigating executive function challenges, information overload, and time blindness, fragmentation becomes a genuine barrier to completion.
research_insights: Manual cross-referencing systems to track hours and competencies—information lives on three platforms that don't communicate.
The official portal is desktop-only; useless on job sites where the actual work happens.
Hours discrepancies create real dread, especially before employer check-ins. Uncertainty has emotional weight.
Inconsistent UI patterns and unclear next steps aren't annoying—they're a cognitive tax paid on every login.

research.content: We reviewed policy and documentation, talked to stakeholders, and developed personas based on lived experience. Core finding: apprentices needed central orientation, not another tool.
methods: Persona development based on lived experience

pivots.from: Solving all apprenticeship problems (financial planning, employer communication, exam prep, tracking)
pivots.reason: Scope creep led to paralysis. One audience, one need. Everything else became clear.

constraints: No access to official SkilledTradesBC APIs
Team learning curve with mobile development

design_direction.intro: Every design choice prioritized one thing: visibility. If apprentices could see where they stood at a glance, confusion dissolved.

lofi.content: Early sketches asked: what information matters most? How quickly can someone find it? Dashboard emerged as central—progress bars, hour counts, next-step prompts. First three seconds must answer 'Where am I?'

hifi.content: High-fidelity design prioritizes calm and clarity. Soft colors, generous spacing, clear typography. Dashboard is the entry point.

design_decisions.rationale: Hours completed, hours remaining, competencies achieved—all visible at a glance, not buried in menus. The answer to 'where am I?' arrives immediately.
Soft colors, generous spacing, clear typography reduce cognitive load. Every visual element earned its space.
Progress tracking is the home screen, not a secondary feature. It's the first thing apprentices see and the reason they open the app.

images.caption: The dashboard provides immediate progress visibility

prototype.description: Browse the high-fidelity wireframes—every screen, component, and interaction state.

features.intro: Four core features, each answering a friction point.

features.title: Progress Dashboard
features.description: Hours completed, hours remaining, competencies achieved, certification progress. The answer to 'where am I?' is always visible.
features.impact: Constant orientation. A GPS for the apprenticeship journey.

features.title: Hours Discrepancy Detector
features.description: Compares user-logged hours against official records and flags mismatches. Users document discrepancies and generate reports.
features.impact: Discrepancies create anxiety. This tool surfaces them so apprentices can advocate for themselves.

features.title: Financial Resources Hub
features.description: Grants, funding programs, and itemized cost breakdowns for apprenticeship expenses. No digging required.
features.impact: Financial information centralized. Everything in one place, not scattered across platforms.

features.title: AI Practice Quizzes
features.description: Generates unique practice questions based on trade competencies. Low-stakes practice before official exams.
features.impact: Confidence building. Each attempt is a step closer to passing.

prototype.description: Start with the dashboard, then explore the hours tracker and quiz flow.

outcome.validation: The design centers on one principle: clarity removes friction. Progress tracking becomes the entry point. Hours discrepancies are surfaced, not hidden.

key_learnings.insight: Clarity is an emotional outcome, not information architecture
key_learnings.reflection: Information alone doesn't create relief. Visibility—removing the need to search—is what produces confidence.

key_learnings.insight: Scope discipline is the hardest design decision
key_learnings.reflection: We had six problem spaces. Choosing one—not the features we built—was the most important design decision we made.
Showing incomplete hours and remaining work creates urgency and clarity. The dashboard is the entry point, not a secondary screen.

next.title: Real-Time Data Sync
next.impact: Hours and milestones update automatically through SkilledTradesBC integration. Removes manual entry—apprentices stay focused on the work.

next.title: Deeper Accessibility
next.impact: Screen reader optimization and high-contrast modes reach all learners. The tool becomes genuinely inclusive, not just compliant.

next.title: Offline-Ready Experience
next.impact: Core features work on job sites with limited connectivity. Progress tracking happens where the work happens—not just where Wi-Fi is.

full_flow_prototype.description: Complete flow—from opening the app, checking progress, logging hours, to preparing for exams. Notice how each screen answers a question before you ask it.

case_studies.title: InkLink
case_studies.subtitle: Breaking writer's block through collaboration
case_studies.summary: A chain-writing platform that reduces writer's block by turning writing into a shared, low-pressure activity where users contribute one passage at a time.
responsibilities: Led user research and concept validation

hook.content:
Everyone has a story they never finished.

Maybe it's a novel that stalled at chapter three. Maybe it's a screenplay that never made it past the outline. The ideas were there—the momentum wasn't.

InkLink started with a simple question: what if writing didn't have to be a solo activity? What if the pressure of finishing was replaced by the fun of contributing?

problem.content:
Writer's block isn't about running out of ideas. It's about pressure. The blank page. Perfectionism. The weight of solo responsibility.

For most writers, solo pressure accumulates. They overthink. They perfect. They never finish. Sharing that weight removes the block.
research_insights: Writers need visible context before contributing—but dense layouts buried the story.
Larger screens were essential; mobile felt claustrophobic for reading passages.
One worked example unlocked confidence; demonstration beats explanation.

research.content:
We talked to amateur and hobbyist writers. Tested early concepts. Validated the premise.

Key finding: Writers don't need a better editor. They need permission to share authorship.
methods: Peer surveys with amateur and hobbyist writers

pivots.reason: Writers consistently preferred larger screens. Mobile felt cramped for both reading and contributing.
Single-column reading lets story carry attention, not interface.

pivots.from: Feature-heavy homepage with multiple entry points
pivots.reason: Homepage with six ways to start? Users chose none. One clear action? Every time.

design_direction.intro: From early wireframes to finished interfaces, every design decision aimed at one thing: removing friction between thought and contribution.

lofi.content:
Lo-fi work focused on the core flow: how to show story history, handle turn-taking, reward brevity.

Simple pattern emerged: read what came before, add your piece, pass it forward.

images.caption: Early exploration of the contribution flow

hifi.content: High-fidelity designs leaned into typography and whitespace. Every design choice served one purpose: let the writing be the focus, not the interface.

design_decisions.rationale: Color used sparingly to indicate action and turn status. Writing itself stayed neutral, letting each voice come through clearly.
Clear reading flow removes scanning. Story carries attention, not interface details.
Contribution happens without hesitation. Gesture, navigation, UI all step back.

images.caption: Clean reading experience with minimal UI
Writing interface with previous context visible

prototype.description: Experience the reading flow. Tap a story to open it, then scroll through contributions.

features.title: Public Story Chains
features.description: Open stories that anyone can contribute to. Writers join, add a passage, and pass the story forward.
features.impact: Low barrier to entry. No commitment—just jump in.

features.title: Private Writing Circles
features.description: Invite-only spaces for friends or writing groups. Turn order set by the creator.
features.impact: Control without strangers. Safety through boundaries.

features.title: Timed Turns
features.description: Optional time limits encourage momentum. If a turn expires, the next writer steps in.
features.impact: Deadlines reduce overthinking. A 24-hour window is fair but keeps stories moving.

prototype.description: Start a new story, invite a friend, or join an existing chain.

outcome.validation: The design removes individual ownership. When writing is shared across multiple contributors, perfectionism loses its grip. Contributions become spontaneous, not labored.

key_learnings.insight: Constraints reduce friction, not increase it
key_learnings.reflection: One clear CTA, timed turns, single-column reading—each constraint removed a decision writers would have otherwise agonized over.
Striking layouts create friction. A clear single-column reading experience lets the story carry attention, not the interface.
The homepage had six ways to start. Users chose none. We cut it to one and they started every time.

next.title: Saved Drafts
next.impact: Writers can step away mid-thought and return without losing momentum.

next.title: Keyboard-First Navigation
next.impact: Full keyboard and screen reader support across the writing flow opens the experience to every writer.

next.title: Guided First Contribution
next.impact: New users understand chain writing through doing, not reading.

full_flow_prototype.description: Complete InkLink experience—from discovering a story to making your first contribution.

case_studies.title: SideQuest
case_studies.subtitle: Turning boredom into micro-adventures
case_studies.summary: A web app that helps users discover small, low-commitment activities to break routine, reframing free time as an opportunity rather than a void to fill.

hook.content:
You have a free afternoon. Nothing planned. No obligations.

So you open your phone, scroll through options, and thirty minutes later you're still on the couch—paralyzed by choice, defaulting to nothing.

SideQuest was designed for that moment. Not to give you more options, but to give you one good one.

problem.content:
Boredom isn't about having nothing to do. It's about choosing what to do.

Endless options create endless paralysis. Flip the model: one suggested thing beats everything you could do.
research_insights: Users seek low-pressure activities that require zero planning—no commitments.
Choice overload causes paralysis and inaction. Too many options = users chose none.
Guided suggestions beat open-ended discovery. Curation reduces friction.
Framing matters. 'Quest' creates adventurous expectation; 'activity' feels obligatory.

research.content: We interviewed peers, tested paper prototypes, and analyzed competitive activity discovery apps. Core finding: the solution wasn't more options—it was removing choice entirely.
methods: Persona creation based on peer interviews
Competitive analysis of activity discovery apps

pivots.from: Discovery app with 5 filters (location, duration, cost, category, tags)
pivots.reason: Every filter was another reason not to act. One button removed every excuse.
Language changes how users relate to an activity before they commit. Adventure > obligation.

pivots.to: Warm palette and friendly mascot guide
pivots.reason: Signals companionship, not logistics solving. Warmth reduces emotional resistance.

constraints: Solo development meant limited feature scope
No backend meant static quest database
Needed to work well on both mobile and desktop

research_diagram.title: App Flows
research_diagram.caption: User journey from idle browsing to quest completion

design_direction.intro: Every design choice reflected one principle: remove friction between boredom and action.

lofi.content:
Early wireframes showed the problem: location, duration, cost, category filters, search bar, 'For You' feed. Too many buttons. Users hovered without committing.

Stripped back to one action: 'Give me a quest.' Everything else became optional.

images.caption: Initial designs with too many options

hifi.content: High-fidelity design leaned into personality. A mascot guides without feeling childish. Playful language and warm colors reduce hesitation.

design_decisions.rationale: 'Give me a quest' removes all decision-making. No filters, no browsing, no deliberation. Just one moment of surrender.
Soft colors, a friendly mascot, playful language. This doesn't feel like a task app—it feels like an invitation.
Framing changes psychology. 'Quest' signals adventure and permission to try; 'activity' sounds like work.

images.caption: The mascot welcomes users with a single clear action
Quest suggestions are simple and actionable
Just enough information to get started

prototype.description: Browse the high-fidelity mockups—every screen, component, and interaction state.

features.intro: Three core mechanics work together to collapse hesitation into action.

features.title: Quest Suggestions
features.description: Small, low-commitment activities. Each includes a title, brief description, and estimated time. No fine print.
features.impact: Framing as 'quest' shifts psychology. Users feel invited, not obligated.

features.title: Friendly Guide
features.description: A small character that appears throughout, offering encouragement without being intrusive.
features.impact: Warmth without manipulation. Personality signals companionship, not persuasion.

features.title: Two-Tap Start
features.description: Get a quest in two taps. No account, no preferences, no setup. Just act.
features.impact: Minimized friction. Between boredom and action, only two decisions stand between.

outcome.validation: The core mechanic proved simple: one button, one suggestion, one moment of decision. Framing and warmth determine whether users act or hesitate.

key_learnings.reflection: One action is worth more than ten options. First quest is hardest. After that, return becomes automatic.

key_learnings.insight: Design reduces hesitation, not increases engagement
key_learnings.reflection: Every visual element either builds or destroys confidence. Celebration is secondary—the act of choosing is the momentum.

next.title: Personalized Quest Feed
next.impact: Suggestions evolve based on completed quests. Each session feels more like yours—less random, more resonant.

next.title: Location-Aware Quests
next.impact: Quests surface based on what's around you right now. Bridges the gap between digital prompt and real-world action.

next.title: Shared Quests
next.impact: Friends take on the same quest and compare. Solo adventures gain a social layer without losing spontaneity.

full_flow_prototype.description: Full project walkthrough—from research through early wireframes to final solution and design decisions.
